statement: The tongue is the most common cause of airway obstruction in unconscious patients.
rationale: In unconscious patients muscle tone is lost and the tongue can fall back against the posterior pharynx blocking the airway. This is why head-tilt chin-lift or jaw thrust maneuvers are effective as they lift the tongue forward.

statement: The epiglottis is a leaf-shaped structure that covers the trachea during swallowing to prevent aspiration.
rationale: The epiglottis is a cartilaginous flap that closes over the glottis opening to the larynx during swallowing. This directs food and liquid into the esophagus and prevents aspiration into the trachea and lungs.

statement: The right main bronchus is more vertical than the left making it the more common site for aspirated foreign objects.
rationale: The right main bronchus branches from the trachea at a less acute angle than the left making it more vertical. Aspirated objects tend to follow gravity and the path of least resistance entering the right bronchus more frequently.

statement: An infant's airway is smaller and more easily obstructed than an adult's airway.
rationale: Infants have proportionally smaller airways with less cartilage support. The tongue is relatively larger compared to the oral cavity. These factors make infants more susceptible to airway obstruction and more sensitive to swelling.

statement: The trachea is protected by complete rings of cartilage along its entire length.
rationale: The trachea is supported by C-shaped cartilage rings not complete rings. The posterior portion is membranous allowing the esophagus which lies behind to expand during swallowing. Only the cricoid cartilage is a complete ring.

statement: Snoring respirations in an unconscious patient indicate partial airway obstruction.
rationale: Snoring or sonorous respirations indicate the tongue or soft tissues are partially obstructing the upper airway. This sound is created by air turbulence as it passes the obstruction. Airway positioning maneuvers are needed.

statement: Gurgling sounds during breathing indicate the presence of fluid in the airway that needs to be suctioned.
rationale: Gurgling indicates liquid such as blood vomit or secretions in the upper airway. This requires immediate suctioning to clear the airway and prevent aspiration. Position changes may also help drain fluid.

statement: Stridor is a high-pitched sound heard during inspiration that indicates upper airway obstruction.
rationale: Stridor is a harsh high-pitched sound typically heard during inspiration caused by turbulent airflow through a narrowed upper airway. Causes include croup epiglottitis foreign body and anaphylaxis. It indicates significant obstruction.

statement: Wheezing is caused by narrowing of the lower airways and is commonly heard in asthma patients.
rationale: Wheezing is a musical whistling sound caused by air moving through narrowed bronchioles. It is characteristic of asthma COPD and allergic reactions. Wheezing is typically heard during expiration but can occur during inspiration in severe cases.

statement: Crackles or rales heard in the lungs indicate fluid in the alveoli often seen in heart failure or pneumonia.
rationale: Crackles are discontinuous popping sounds heard during inspiration caused by air passing through fluid in the small airways and alveoli. They are common in pulmonary edema from heart failure and in pneumonia.

statement: The head-tilt chin-lift is the preferred method for opening the airway in patients with suspected spinal injury.
rationale: Head-tilt chin-lift extends the cervical spine and is contraindicated when spinal injury is suspected. The jaw-thrust maneuver opens the airway by displacing the mandible forward without moving the neck.

statement: The jaw-thrust maneuver opens the airway by moving the mandible forward which lifts the tongue off the posterior pharynx.
rationale: The jaw thrust displaces the lower jaw anteriorly which pulls the tongue forward and away from the back of the throat. This opens the airway without requiring neck extension making it safe for trauma patients.

statement: Hyperextension of the neck is recommended for opening the airway in infants.
rationale: Infants have large occiputs that naturally flex the neck when supine and their airways are more pliable. The neutral or sniffing position with slight extension is recommended. Hyperextension can actually kink and obstruct an infant's airway.

statement: An oropharyngeal airway OPA is used to prevent the tongue from obstructing the airway in unconscious patients.
rationale: The OPA is a rigid curved device inserted into the mouth that holds the tongue away from the posterior pharynx. It maintains airway patency in unconscious patients who do not have a gag reflex.

statement: An OPA should only be used in patients who do not have a gag reflex.
rationale: Inserting an OPA in a patient with an intact gag reflex can stimulate vomiting and aspiration. The OPA is only appropriate for deeply unconscious patients. If the patient gags during insertion remove it immediately.

statement: The correct OPA size is measured from the corner of the mouth to the angle of the jaw or earlobe.
rationale: Proper OPA sizing ensures the device is long enough to hold the tongue forward but not so long that it pushes the epiglottis closed. Measure from the corner of the mouth to the earlobe or angle of the jaw.

statement: In adults the OPA is inserted with the tip pointing upward then rotated 180 degrees as it passes the tongue.
rationale: Adult OPA insertion involves inserting with the tip toward the palate then rotating 180 degrees as the device passes the tongue. This prevents pushing the tongue backward. In children insert with tip pointing down without rotation.

statement: A nasopharyngeal airway NPA can be used in patients who have an intact gag reflex.
rationale: The NPA is better tolerated than the OPA in patients with intact gag reflex or those who are not deeply unconscious. It is inserted through the nose and passes behind the tongue to maintain the airway.

statement: An NPA is contraindicated in patients with suspected basilar skull fracture or severe facial trauma.
rationale: In basilar skull fracture the cribriform plate may be fractured. An NPA could potentially enter the cranial cavity through this defect. Signs of basilar skull fracture include raccoon eyes Battle sign and CSF leakage from nose or ears.

statement: The NPA should be lubricated with petroleum-based jelly before insertion.
rationale: Water-soluble lubricant should be used for NPA insertion. Petroleum-based products like Vaseline can damage the device and if aspirated can cause lipoid pneumonia. Always use water-soluble lubricant.

statement: Suctioning should be limited to 10-15 seconds to prevent hypoxia.
rationale: Suctioning removes air as well as secretions. Prolonged suctioning can cause hypoxia. Limit each suction attempt to 10-15 seconds and allow the patient to breathe or be ventilated between attempts.

statement: A rigid suction catheter like the Yankauer is best for suctioning thick secretions and vomit from the oropharynx.
rationale: Rigid catheters like the Yankauer are designed for removing thick secretions blood clots and vomit from the mouth and oropharynx. They are more effective than flexible catheters for these materials.

statement: When suctioning an adult you should insert the catheter as far as possible to remove all secretions.
rationale: Insert the rigid suction catheter only as far as you can see in the oropharynx. Blind deep insertion can cause trauma vomiting or laryngospasm. Suction only visible material in the mouth and throat.

statement: The bag-valve-mask BVM can deliver nearly 100% oxygen when connected to supplemental oxygen.
rationale: When connected to oxygen at 15 LPM with a reservoir bag the BVM can deliver approximately 90-100% oxygen concentration. Without supplemental oxygen it delivers only room air at 21% oxygen.

statement: Two-person BVM ventilation is more effective than one-person technique.
rationale: With two rescuers one can use both hands to maintain mask seal and airway position while the other squeezes the bag. This results in better tidal volumes and less air leak compared to single-rescuer technique.

statement: The proper ventilation rate for an adult using BVM is 10-12 breaths per minute.
rationale: Adult ventilation rate with BVM is 10-12 breaths per minute or one breath every 5-6 seconds. Faster rates cause gastric insufflation and reduce venous return to the heart impairing circulation.

statement: Each BVM ventilation should deliver as much volume as possible to ensure adequate oxygenation.
rationale: Excessive ventilation volume causes gastric insufflation increases aspiration risk and impairs venous return. Deliver just enough volume to see the chest rise which is approximately 500-600 mL in adults.

statement: Gastric distension during BVM ventilation indicates the ventilations are being delivered too fast or with too much volume.
rationale: Gastric insufflation occurs when air enters the stomach instead of the lungs. This results from excessive volume rapid delivery or poor airway positioning. Correct by repositioning and using slower smaller breaths.

statement: A nasal cannula can deliver 24-44% oxygen at flow rates of 1-6 liters per minute.
rationale: The nasal cannula is a low-flow device that delivers approximately 24% oxygen at 1 LPM increasing about 4% per liter up to approximately 44% at 6 LPM. Higher flows dry the nasal mucosa and provide little additional benefit.

statement: A non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM can deliver approximately 90% oxygen to the patient.
rationale: The non-rebreather mask with reservoir bag at 15 LPM delivers high-concentration oxygen of approximately 90%. One-way valves prevent exhaled air from entering the reservoir ensuring high oxygen concentration with each breath.

statement: The reservoir bag on a non-rebreather mask should be fully inflated before placing the mask on the patient.
rationale: The reservoir bag should be filled with oxygen before use by occluding the one-way valve or using the oxygen flush. If the bag is empty the patient will not receive adequate oxygen on the first breaths.

statement: Oxygen should never be given to patients with COPD because it will cause them to stop breathing.
rationale: Never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic patient regardless of COPD status. While some COPD patients may have reduced respiratory drive with high oxygen the risk of hypoxia is more immediately life-threatening. Titrate oxygen to maintain adequate saturation.

statement: Blow-by oxygen is an appropriate technique for young children who will not tolerate a mask.
rationale: Children who become distressed with masks on their face may tolerate blow-by oxygen where the mask or tubing is held near the face. A calm child receiving blow-by often maintains better oxygenation than a crying child fighting a mask.

statement: Oxygen supports combustion and should be kept away from open flames and sparks.
rationale: While oxygen itself does not burn it greatly accelerates combustion. Materials that would smolder in room air can burst into flame in an oxygen-enriched environment. Keep oxygen away from flames sparks and heat sources.

statement: Oxygen cylinders should be stored lying flat to prevent them from falling.
rationale: Oxygen cylinders should be stored upright and secured to prevent falling. A falling cylinder can damage the valve potentially causing the cylinder to become a dangerous projectile if the valve breaks.

statement: Pulse oximetry measures the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen.
rationale: Pulse oximetry SpO2 uses light absorption to measure the percentage of hemoglobin molecules carrying oxygen. Normal SpO2 is 94-100% on room air. Values below 94% indicate hypoxia requiring supplemental oxygen.

statement: Pulse oximetry is always accurate and has no limitations.
rationale: Pulse oximetry can be inaccurate with poor perfusion excessive movement carbon monoxide poisoning dark nail polish severe anemia and hypothermia. It also measures saturation not oxygen delivery to tissues.

statement: Carbon monoxide poisoning may show normal or high SpO2 readings despite severe hypoxia.
rationale: Pulse oximeters cannot distinguish between oxyhemoglobin and carboxyhemoglobin. A patient with severe CO poisoning may have a normal SpO2 reading while their tissues are starving for oxygen. Clinical assessment is essential.

statement: The universal sign of choking is clutching the throat with one or both hands.
rationale: The universal choking sign of hands to throat indicates airway obstruction and should prompt immediate action. A choking victim who cannot speak cough or breathe needs immediate intervention.

statement: Abdominal thrusts Heimlich maneuver are used to relieve choking in conscious adults and children over one year of age.
rationale: Abdominal thrusts create artificial coughing by forcing air up through the trachea to expel foreign objects. This technique is used for conscious choking victims over one year old who cannot cough speak or breathe.

statement: Back blows and chest thrusts are used to relieve choking in infants under one year of age.
rationale: Infants under one year receive alternating back blows and chest thrusts rather than abdominal thrusts. Abdominal thrusts can damage infant internal organs. Give 5 back blows then 5 chest thrusts repeating until object is expelled.

statement: If a choking victim becomes unconscious you should immediately begin CPR.
rationale: When a choking victim becomes unconscious begin CPR starting with chest compressions. Compressions may help dislodge the obstruction. Look in the mouth before each breath attempt and remove any visible object. Do not perform blind finger sweeps.

statement: Blind finger sweeps should be performed in unconscious choking victims to locate and remove the object.
rationale: Blind finger sweeps are not recommended as they can push the object deeper into the airway. Only remove objects you can see. Begin CPR and look in the mouth before each ventilation attempt.

statement: A patient with partial airway obstruction who is coughing forcefully should receive abdominal thrusts immediately.
rationale: A patient who is coughing effectively has air exchange and may be able to clear the obstruction themselves. Encourage continued coughing and do not interfere. Intervene with thrusts only if coughing becomes ineffective or the patient cannot breathe.

statement: Patients with tracheostomies breathe through the stoma in their neck not through the nose and mouth.
rationale: A tracheostomy is a surgical opening in the trachea. Patients with tracheostomies breathe through this stoma. Some patients with laryngectomy total removal of larynx cannot breathe through their nose or mouth at all.

statement: Ventilation for a patient with a tracheostomy should be provided through the stoma not the mouth.
rationale: For tracheostomy patients ventilate through the stoma using a pediatric mask or special adapter. For total laryngectomy patients seal the mouth and nose to prevent air leak as all breathing is through the stoma.

statement: Suctioning the tracheostomy tube can help clear mucus that is obstructing the airway.
rationale: Mucus plugging is common in tracheostomy patients. Suctioning the tracheostomy tube can clear secretions and restore airway patency. Use appropriate suction catheters and sterile technique when possible.

statement: Tripod position sitting upright leaning forward with hands on knees is commonly assumed by patients in respiratory distress.
rationale: Tripod position maximizes the mechanical advantage of breathing muscles especially the diaphragm. Patients instinctively assume this position when in respiratory distress. Allow them to remain in position of comfort.

statement: Use of accessory muscles in the neck indicates increased work of breathing and respiratory distress.
rationale: Accessory muscles including the sternocleidomastoid scalenes and intercostals are not normally used for quiet breathing at rest. Their visible use indicates the patient is working hard to breathe and may be in respiratory distress.

statement: Nasal flaring and intercostal retractions in children are signs of respiratory distress.
rationale: Nasal flaring widening of nostrils and retractions visible pulling in between ribs or above the clavicles indicate increased work of breathing in children. These are important signs of respiratory distress requiring intervention.
